title: Stage One Purpose at a Time
summary: Separate supplied community-site edits so each commit records one understandable project purpose.

sketchBlocks.title: Look at unstaged text changes before you stage
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: You receive a small design tweak and a content update in the same folder. In community-site/index.html the contributor added a new paragraph under the headline; in community-site/styles.css someone adjusted spacing. Before staging anything, check exactly what changed so you don't accidentally group unrelated edits.

Run git status to see changed files. Then run git diff to view line-level unstaged edits. Typical commands:

$ git status
$ git diff community-site/index.html

The git diff output shows which lines were added (a plus) or removed (a minus). In this example the diff shows one added paragraph in index.html and several style-rule changes in styles.css. Because you only want the content update in this commit, stage only index.html with:

$ git add community-site/index.html

Now run git diff --staged to view the staged snapshot. It will show the same added paragraph but not the style-rule changes (those remain unstaged). This separation ensures the next commit documents only the content change and leaves the styling edits for a later, separate commit.

Interpretation: Seeing the staged diff guarantees the commit will contain precisely the intended lines. If the staged snapshot included unrelated edits, you'd unstage them (git restore --staged) before committing.

sketchBlocks.title: Choose files to stage so each commit has one purpose
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: The project needs two focused changes: a typo fix in community-site/about.txt and a new sample image added to community-site/images/. Both were edited in the same working session. To keep history understandable, stage the typo fix alone and leave the images addition unstaged.

Start by listing changed files with git status. Stage only the about.txt change:

$ git add community-site/about.txt

Then confirm the staged snapshot contains only the about change:

$ git diff --staged -- community-site/about.txt

Because the images remain unstaged, they will not be part of this commit. Committing now with a concise message like "Fix typo in About page" creates a focused commit that documents the single purpose of the change.

sketchBlocks.title: Verify staged snapshot, write a focused message, and commit
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After staging the relevant files (for example, community-site/index.html and community-site/about.txt for content edits), verify the snapshot and compose a focused commit.

Verify staged content:

$ git diff --staged

This shows exactly what will enter the next commit. Avoid committing if unrelated edits are present. When the staged snapshot looks right, create a focused commit with a clear single-line message:

$ git commit -m "Update community copy: add paragraph and fix contact wording"

After the commit, run git status and git log --oneline to confirm the working tree is clean and the history contains the focused message. The habit: stage, inspect staged diff, commit with a single purpose message.

sketchBlocks.title: Use log and show to answer questions about the last commit
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Worked example: After making two focused commits (a content commit and a style commit), use git log --oneline to see concise history and git show to inspect a particular commit's snapshot.

List recent commits:

$ git log --oneline -n 5

Pick the commit you're curious about (for example, the second newest) and inspect it:

$ git show <that-commit>

git show prints the commit metadata and the diff that the commit introduced, letting you answer questions such as "Which file added the new paragraph?" or "What changed in about.txt?". Reading compact history and using git show helps you verify that each commit documents a clear purpose and that the repository history is readable.

quizDraft.title: What's the main difference between git diff and git diff --staged?
options: git diff shows unstaged working-tree changes; git diff --staged shows what is staged and will be committed.
git diff lists only filenames changed; git diff --staged shows full line-level changes.
git diff only works on untracked files; git diff --staged only works on tracked files.
git diff always shows committed history; git diff --staged shows uncommitted edits.
quizDraft.hint: Think about whether the command shows changes in the working tree or the staged snapshot.

help.concept: git diff shows unstaged working-tree differences; git diff --staged (aka --cached) shows the differences between HEAD and the staged snapshot that will become the next commit.
help.hint_1: Remember the staging area holds what will go into the next commit. git diff --staged inspects that snapshot.
help.hint_2: If you want to see exactly what will be committed, use git diff --staged rather than git diff.

quizDraft.title: When should you create separate commits instead of one combined commit?
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all options that are valid reasons to keep changes in separate commits.
options: The edits change unrelated concerns (content vs. styling) and should be separated.
One set of changes is a small copy fix while another is adding many new files (media); separate them.
You always stage everything and make a single big commit to reduce the number of commits.
You want each commit message to document a single, understandable purpose for reviewers and future you.
quizDraft.hint: Think about readability and the purpose of each change.

help.concept: Good commit granularity groups changes by single, coherent purpose; separate commits help revert, understand, and review changes later.
help.hint_1: Commit messages and diffs should map to a single intention (e.g., fix a bug, update copy, adjust layout).
help.hint_2: Separate commits are useful when changes affect different concerns, like content vs. media, or when one change is risky and likely to be reverted.

quizDraft.title: What information does git show <commit> display? (Choose all that apply)
quizDraft.prompt: Select every true statement about the output of git show for a specific commit.
options: The commit's message and author information.
A diff showing what lines the commit added or removed.
A list of all files currently in the repository, regardless of the commit.
The patch that was introduced by that specific commit.
quizDraft.hint: Think about metadata and the commit's diff.

help.concept: git show prints the commit metadata (author, date, message) and the patch (diff) introduced by that commit; it does not list unrelated commits unless asked.
help.hint_1: Look for answers referencing the commit message, author, and the diff of files changed in that commit.
help.hint_2: git show does not show the entire project's file contents at arbitrary commits in a single summary unless you ask for tree listings.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps for creating a focused commit
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the four steps in the correct order to create a focused commit from local edits.
tokens: Stage only the files related to one purpose (git add specific-file)
Inspect unstaged working-tree changes (git status, git diff)
Commit the staged snapshot with a focused message (git commit -m "...")
Verify the staged snapshot shows only the intended changes (git diff --staged)
correctOrder: Inspect unstaged working-tree changes (git status, git diff)
Stage only the files related to one purpose (git add specific-file)
Verify the staged snapshot shows only the intended changes (git diff --staged)
Commit the staged snapshot with a focused message (git commit -m "...")
quizDraft.hint: Think: inspect, stage only the right files, verify staged snapshot, then commit.

help.concept: A focused commit comes from inspecting changes, staging only the files for one purpose, verifying the staged snapshot, and then committing with a clear message.
help.hint_1: Start by inspecting what changed in the working tree, not by immediately staging everything.
help.hint_2: Use git diff and git diff --staged at the appropriate times to confirm what will be committed.

quizDraft.title: Complete the command to view what is currently staged
quizDraft.prompt:
Choose the token that fills the blank so the command shows only the staged snapshot:

git [blank1] --staged
quizDraft.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

quizDraft.title: Pick the best short commit message for a focused change
quizDraft.prompt:
Choose the best message to fill the blank for a commit that only fixes a spelling mistake in About page:

Commit message: "[blank1]"
choices: Misc edits including content and styles
quizDraft.hint: The message should be concise and describe a single purpose.

help.concept: A focused commit message briefly describes the single purpose of the change, typically in present-tense imperative style.
help.hint_1: Avoid vague messages like "misc updates" or unrelated descriptions.
help.hint_2: Prefer short messages like "the missing term".

quizDraft.title: Stage only the content change, not the styling change
quizDraft.prompt:
You are working in the community-site folder. Hidden setup placed two unstaged edits: community-site/header.txt now has a corrected headline line, and community-site/styles.css has unrelated spacing changes. Use the terminal to: (1) inspect status, (2) view the unstaged diff for header.txt, (3) stage only community-site/header.txt, (4) confirm the staged snapshot shows the header change but not the styles change, and (5) commit the staged snapshot with the message: "Fix headline text in header.txt". Type each command and press Enter. After committing, the repository should be clean.

Why this matters: You keep the content fix separate from styling edits so future readers understand the change purpose.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.
Community site starter files for the guided lesson.

solutionFiles.content:
# Inspect working tree
git status
# View the unstaged change for the header file
git diff community-site/header.txt
# Stage only the header fix
git add community-site/header.txt
# Verify staged snapshot contains the header change but not styles.css
git diff --staged
# Commit the staged snapshot with the required message
git commit -m "Fix headline text in header.txt"
# Confirm the working tree is clean
git status

quizDraft.title: Stage a small typo fix and leave media additions unstaged
quizDraft.prompt:
Hidden setup added two changes in community-site: (1) a one-line typo fix inside community-site/about.txt and (2) a new image file community-site/images/event.jpg (not yet tracked). Use the terminal to: inspect changes, stage only community-site/about.txt, confirm the staged diff shows only the typo fix, commit with message "Fix typo in About page", and verify that the new image file is still unstaged/untracked after the commit. Type each command and press Enter.

Why this matters: Media files often belong to a separate commit or workflow; keep the text fix separate so history stays focused.

git add community-site/about.txt
# Verify staged snapshot
git diff --staged
# Commit with focused message
git commit -m "Fix typo in About page"
# Confirm new image is still untracked
git status

quizDraft.title: Verify staged snapshot and commit with a focused message
quizDraft.prompt: Hidden setup provided two staged changes: community-site/contact.txt (staged) and community-site/notes.txt (unstaged). Your task in the terminal: (1) show the staged snapshot only, (2) if the staged snapshot contains exactly the contact change, commit it with message "Update contact info", and (3) leave notes.txt unstaged. Use git diff --staged and git commit -m. Type each command and press Enter. After committing, confirm the working tree still includes the unstaged notes.txt change.

# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.

# Commit the staged snapshot with the specified message
git commit -m "Update contact info"
# Verify notes.txt is still unstaged
git status
# Optionally view unstaged diff for notes.txt
git diff community-site/notes.txt

quizDraft.title: Answer history questions using log and show after making a focused commit
quizDraft.prompt: Hidden setup created a repository state with one unstaged change to community-site/footer.txt. Stage and commit only the footer change with message "Adjust footer copy". Then run git log --oneline -n 3 and use git show on the newest commit to confirm which file changed and what the commit message contains. Type each command and press Enter. Finally, report (by viewing git log and git show) that the newest commit message exactly matches "Adjust footer copy" and that the commit modified community-site/footer.txt.
quizDraft.starterCode:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.

starterFiles.content:
# Work in the terminal; the starting repository state is ready.
# Type each requested command and press Enter.

solutionFiles.content:
# Stage the footer change
git add community-site/footer.txt
# Commit with the required message
git commit -m "Adjust footer copy"
# Show concise history
git log --oneline -n 3
# Inspect the newest commit
git show HEAD
# Confirm message and that community-site/footer.txt was changed
